comments.text:

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you for watching — glad you enjoyed it.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Bali looks like nowhere else on earth — one of those places that photographs like a dream and still manages to exceed it. More journeys on the way.

comments.author: @ZY-CO
comments.text: I arrived in Bali one day before the Covid lockdown and ended up spending 15 months there. A lot was closed and there were restrictions, but I got to see it without all the tourists. It was one of the amazing times of my life. Bali is incredible, but it has many faces and you need to find the real Bali, you won't find it in the south. Get out on a motorcycle and drive around the centre towards the north and east, it's stunning beyond words. If you're there in the tourist season, don't just focus on the highlights, there's so many hidden gems everywhere.

replies.author: @Vee_of_the_Weald
replies.text:
I was lucky enough to go to Bali in 2002, before Julia Roberts and before the South of the island became a circus. We hired a tour guide and insisted that we wanted to see the real Bali. He took us all over the island, to small villages and told us the history of his people. For me, the Balinese are integral to the beauty of Bali. 
I remember waking up with a view over the paddy fields somewhere in the north east and a little boy playing nearby. We politely smiled at each other. It was incredible!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fifteen months of Bali with the tourist layer stripped away — that's the film we were trying to make. The north and east are exactly where the real story lives. Thank you for sharing that. 🌿

comments.author: @jukkasunborn
comments.text:
Execptionally good video about Bali. You guys did your homework well. Even mentioned the -65, about what most of the younger generations of Balinese know nothing about. There is a project going on now, where they are collecting and archiving the memories of people who went through it.
Bali is not - and have never been -  The Island of gods, it is still the island of thousands of evil spirits, and the Balinese are still scared to death of them. 
PS. the peKalang hurt my ears. 😁

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That correction deserves to be heard — the "Island of Gods" branding papers over something far more complex and genuinely felt. The spirit world there isn't decorative. Thank you for watching. 🌿

comments.author: @susanastump4719
comments.text: This is very interesting. Thank you. I wonder how to find hiking tours in Bali? I imagine that is the best way to see the island.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Bali's interior is where the island really reveals itself — the rice terraces, volcanic trails around Gunung Batur, and village paths above Munduk are a world away from the coast. Which side of the island are you most drawn to?

comments.author: @虎瀬野
comments.text: make sense if karangasem and singaraja architecture stained by european style....now the most pure and neat balinese puri only in ubud and peliatan...which in inland part of island and one of last conquered...same case for kratons in java....because there were only 2 main kratons...all heavily tainted by european style.. it went to other parts of vernacular architecture too..became so messed up....till now...the walls, fences, gates, towers, pillars, layout, everything else

replies.author: @jukkasunborn
replies.text: There are dozens of Puri around Bali that are still in perfect examples of Balinese architecture. More so than the many puris of Ubud  and Peliatan., which all have modern constructions inside them,

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The inland-versus-coast divide as an architectural timeline of contact — that's the kind of layer most visitors never think to look for. Thank you for that. 🌿

comments.author: @Selma672
comments.text: Bali is an beautiful island. Thank you for the video.🤗🤗

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Glad you enjoyed it! Thank you for watching! More documentaries to come🤗

comments.author: @MammaBear-xp7ur
comments.text: Unfortunately Bali is slowly being taken over by Islam. More and more shops and businesses are Muslim.  It should be left for the Hindus, they need their own space untouched.  It’s a beautiful island, once that balance tips, tourism will be crushed.

comments.author: @MalalaTinaKovacic
comments.text: You make me cry. Thank you for this video❤

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That kind of response is what every frame was reaching for. Thank you for watching. 🌿

comments.author: @Made157-d7q
comments.text: what is happening in Bali now is evil the destruction of an ancient culture by a culture of greed and selfishness

replies.author: @jukkasunborn
replies.text: @WhereonEarth4K The silence of Nyepi was almost gone few years ago, when tourists were driving around on guidd tours and Balinese kids were revving their motorbikes on carless streets. Then the governement and villages took a hardes stand and now we can wnjoy the quietness.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: It's a real tension, and you're feeling the same thing the documentary kept circling back to. The deeper Bali is still there — in the offerings, the calendar, the silence of Nyepi. The question is how much of it the next century can hold. Thank you for watching so closely.

comments.author: @Aventurex-686
comments.text: 29:06 These videos really show that beyond Bali, Indonesia still has so many incredible places and landscapes we haven’t fully discovered yet. Thanks for sharing such impressive footage and showing a different side of the country.

replies.author: @ameliasukh8044
replies.text: None is as beautiful as Bali.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Indonesia has a way of making the famous part feel like just the opening chapter. What in the film made you want to look further?

comments.author: @MeridianQuest
comments.text: I grew up never thinking I'd ever travel. Videos like this keep the dream alive. Thank you for making this.

replies.author: @samsarakamikazegirl
replies.text: Treat travel like a series of bills you pay to yourself. Sketch out a timeline/budget. Spread it out over eight months. Buy the flights as one bill, then the accomodation in bits n pieces online. That’s how I started out.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That means a great deal — keeping that door open is exactly why these films exist. Is there a destination from the channel that's moved to the top of that list?
